content: Before a single curve goes into Illustrator, I draw the wordmark by hand. Pencil first, then a brush pen for weight. The screen flattens decisions you should still be feeling out on paper. A logotype that started as a real gesture keeps a little of that warmth, even after it is vectored and tightened.
excerpt: Pencil before pixels: how a hand-drawn wordmark keeps its warmth.
title: Why I draw every wordmark by hand first

content: A ductus is just the order and direction of your strokes. Lock it down once and your letters stop fighting each other. I keep a single reference sheet per alphabet: entry angle, thin-to-thick transitions, where the pressure releases. Consistency is not the enemy of character; it is what lets character read.
excerpt: Set the ductus once and your letters stop fighting each other.
title: Building a flexible brush-lettering ductus

content: Harbor needed labels that felt small-batch without reading as precious. We landed on a hand-lettered logotype in warm ink, paired with a quiet grotesque for the scent names. Earth tones throughout. The brief asked for the word coastal; the answer was restraint, an ampersand I loved drawing, and a lot of white space.
excerpt: A hand-lettered logotype, a quiet grotesque, and a great deal of restraint.
title: Case study: refreshing the Harbor Candle Co. labels

content: Five minutes of ovals and parallel lines before any client work. It is unglamorous and it is the whole game. The warm-up is where I find the day's rhythm and shed yesterday's bad habits. Lettering rewards the artist who shows up to the boring part.
excerpt: Five minutes of ovals before anything else. It is the whole game.
title: On the discipline of the warm-up sheet

content: Kerning gets talked about as intuition, and it is, but intuition you can train. I letter a test word, blur my eyes, and look for the dark spots. Even rhythm of light and dark across a word is the goal. Trust the squint test more than the numbers.
excerpt: Blur your eyes and chase even rhythm. The squint test rarely lies.
title: Spacing is a feeling you can measure

content: A quieter season let me rebuild the print library and tidy the brush archive. New letterpress stock arrived, and I finally retired three pens that had served their time. Small housekeeping, but the studio feels lighter for it.
excerpt: Rebuilt the print library, retired three tired pens, lighter for it.
title: Studio notes: what changed this winter

content: When we started Harbor, the labels were a stopgap. Working with Laura, we wanted packaging that matched the care in the pour. The hand-lettered marks gave each scent its own voice while keeping the shelf cohesive. Customers notice. So do we, every time we box an order.
excerpt: Packaging that finally matches the care in the pour.
title: Harbor Candle: notes from the founder on our rebrand

content: Most monograms fail because the letters merely sit beside each other. Real interlock means shared strokes and negative space doing double duty. I sketch dozens before one clicks. When it does, the two letters read as a single considered mark, not a collision.
excerpt: Shared strokes, negative space doing double duty, dozens of sketches.
title: Drafting a monogram that actually interlocks

content: I came in with mood boards and left with a system. Laura translated a loose feeling into a logotype, a palette, and a set of rules I could actually follow. The hand-lettered logo still surprises me. It looks like the work feels.
excerpt: A loose feeling, translated into a logotype I can actually use.
title: Working with Laura on the styling brand identity

content: Loud lettering wants a calm companion. I usually reach for a humanist sans or a low-contrast serif that gets out of the way. The lettering carries the personality; the body face carries the reading. Let each do its one job well.
excerpt: Loud lettering wants a calm companion that gets out of the way.
title: Choosing a body face to pair with hand lettering

content: This one is still in progress. A longer piece on building a personal type-specimen, the kind you actually flip through when a project stalls. Posting the outline so I stop circling it.
excerpt: Notes toward a personal type-specimen you would actually use.
title: A draft worth sitting with before publishing
